output:
No. The clock uncertainty does not change the determination.

**Raw timestamps:** The difference is **34 milliseconds** (19:30:00.123 − 19:30:00.089), well within the 100ms window.

**Worst-case uncertainty analysis:**

| Source | Magnitude |
|---|---|
| London GPS propagation delay | 12 µs (0.012 ms) |
| New York atomic clock accuracy | ±1 µs (0.001 ms) |
| **Combined worst case** | **~0.013 ms** |

Even combining both uncertainties maximally in either direction, the true difference lies in the range **~33.987 ms to ~34.013 ms**. That range is entirely within the 100ms window — not remotely close to the boundary.

The uncertainty would only matter if the raw difference were within ~0.013ms of the 100ms threshold (i.e., between roughly 99.987ms and 100.013ms). At 34ms, you're off by nearly **66ms** from the boundary, so microsecond-level clock errors are irrelevant to the wash-trading determination.
